title: Limits of piecewise functions
description: Given the rules for a piecewise function, find limits of that function.
topic_path: Determining limits using algebraic properties of limits: direct substitution

hints: $x=4$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}f(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $4$ from the left. We will use the fact that $f(x)=2^x$ for $x$-values smaller than $4$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^-}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4^-}2^x
\\\\
&=2^4&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=16
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $16$.

This means that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}f(x)=16$.
$x=-1$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}g(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-1$ from the left. We will use the fact that $g(x)=\dfrac1x$ for $x$-values smaller than $-1$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}\dfrac1x
\\\\
&=\dfrac{1}{-1}&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-1$ from the right. We will use the fact that $g(x)=1+2x$ for $x$-values greater than $-1$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^+}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^+}1+2x
\\\\
&=1+2(-1)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $-1$.

This means that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1}g(x)=-1$.
$x=-2$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}h(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-2$ from the left. We will use the fact that $h(x)=5x$ for $x$-values smaller than $-2$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^-}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^-}5x
\\\\
&=5(-2)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-10
\end{align}$

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-2$ from the right. We will use the fact that $h(x)=x^3-2$ for $x$-values greater than $-2$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}x^3-2
\\\\
&=(-2)^3-2&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-10
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $-10$.

This means that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}h(x)=-10$.
$x=\pi$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi}f(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $\pi$ from the left. We will use the fact that $f(x)=\text{sin}(x)$ for $x$-values smaller than $\pi$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^-}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^-}\text{sin}(x)
\\\\
&=\text{sin}(\pi)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $\pi$ from the right. We will use the fact that $f(x)=\dfrac x\pi-1$ for $x$-values greater than $\pi$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^+}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^+}\dfrac x\pi-1
\\\\
&=\dfrac\pi\pi-1&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$

The one-sided limits are both equal to $0$.

This means that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi}f(x)=0$.
$x=2$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}g(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $2$ from the left. We will use the fact that $g(x)=\text{ln}(x)$ for $x$-values smaller than $2$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^-}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^-}\text{ln}(x)
\\\\
&=\text{ln}(2)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\end{align}$

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $2$ from the right. We will use the fact that $g(x)=x^2\text{ln}(2)$ for $x$-values greater than $2$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^+}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2^+}x^2\text{ln}(2)
\\\\
&=2^2\cdot\text{ln}(2)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=4\cdot\text{ln}(2)
\end{align}$

$\text{ln}(2)\neq 4\cdot\text{ln}(2)$ so the one-sided limits aren't equal.

This means that the two-sided limit $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 2}g(x)$ doesn't exist.
$x=-2$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}h(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-2$ from the right. We will use the fact that $h(x)=2^x$ for $x$-values greater than $-2$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2^+}2^x
\\\\
&=2^{-2}&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=\dfrac14
\end{align}$

$-\dfrac14\neq \dfrac14$ so the one-sided limits aren't equal.

This means that the two-sided limit $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -2}h(x)$ doesn't exist.
$x=-3$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}f(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-3$ from the right. We will use the fact that $f(x)=x^2-5$ for $x$-values greater than $-3$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^+}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3^+}x^2-5
\\\\
&=(-3)^2-5&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=4
\end{align}$

$2\neq 4$ so the one-sided limits aren't equal.

This means that the two-sided limit $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}f(x)$ doesn't exist.
$x=6$ is on the boundary between the pieces of our piecewise function. In order to find $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6}g(x)$, we need to find the one-sided limits.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $6$ from the left. We will use the fact that $g(x)=\dfrac x3-2$ for $x$-values smaller than $6$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^-}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^-}\dfrac x3-2
\\\\
&=\dfrac63-2&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $6$ from the right. We will use the fact that $g(x)=\text{cos}(x\cdot\pi)$ for $x$-values greater than $6$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^+}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6^+}\text{cos}(x\cdot\pi)
\\\\
&=\text{cos}(6\pi)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=1
\end{align}$

$0\neq 1$ so the one-sided limits aren't equal.

This means that the two-sided limit $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 6}g(x)$ doesn't exist.
Notice that we were asked to find the one-sided limit, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^+}h(x)$. This is the limit where $x$-values approach $\pi$ from the right.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $\pi$ from the right. We will use the fact that $h(x)=\text{sin}(x)$ for $x$-values greater than $\pi$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^+}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^+}\text{sin}(x)
\\\\
&=\text{sin}(\pi)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to \pi^+}h(x)=0$.
Notice that we were asked to find the one-sided limit, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^+}f(x)$. This is the limit where $x$-values approach $-4$ from the right.
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -4^+}f(x)=\dfrac{1}{16}$.
Notice that we were asked to find the one-sided limit, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}g(x)$. This is the limit where $x$-values approach $-1$ from the left.
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -1^-}g(x)=\dfrac{1}{e}$.
Notice that we were asked to find the one-sided limit, $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^-}h(x)$. This is the limit where $x$-values approach $3$ from the left.

Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $3$ from the left. We will use the fact that $h(x)=x^2-1$ for $x$-values smaller than $3$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^-}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^-}x^2-1
\\\\
&=(3)^2-1&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=8
\end{align}$
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 3^-}h(x)=8$.
Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $1$. We will use the fact that $f(x)=\text{ln}(x)$ for $x$-values larger than $0$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}\text{ln}(x)
\\\\
&=\text{ln}(1)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 1}f(x)=0$.
Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $4$. We will use the fact that $g(x)=\sqrt x$ for $x$-values larger than $1$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}\sqrt x
\\\\
&=\sqrt4&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=2
\end{align}$
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to 4}g(x)=2$.
Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-3$. We will use the fact that $h(x)=2x+1$ for $x$-values smaller than $-2$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}2x+1
\\\\
&=2(-3)+1&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=-5
\end{align}$
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -3}h(x)=-5$.
Let's find the limit as $x$ approaches $-5$. We will use the fact that $f(x)=\text{sin}(x\cdot\pi)$ for $x$-values between $-8$ and $0$.

$\begin{align}
&\phantom{=}\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5}f(x)
\\\\
&=\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5}\text{sin}(x\cdot\pi)
\\\\
&=\text{sin}(-5\pi)&\gray{\text{Direct substitution}}
\\\\
&=0
\end{align}$
In conclusion, we found that $\displaystyle\lim_{x\to -5}f(x)=0$.
